title: Principles of Mathematical Analysis
excerpt: "Baby Rudin": the classic undergraduate text in real analysis. Rigorous, elegant, and demanding — the standard against which all other analysis texts are measured.
whoShouldRead: Undergraduate mathematics majors after a proof-based calculus course
Graduate students needing a rigorous foundation in real analysis
Self-learners with strong proof experience seeking depth in analysis
Instructors designing a demanding undergraduate analysis sequence
whoShouldSkip: First-time calculus students without proof experience
Those needing computational practice or applied analysis
Readers intimidated by dense, concise epsilon-delta proofs
keyIdeas: Real and complex number systems as complete ordered fields
Basic topology of metric spaces: open/closed sets, compactness, connectedness
Sequences, series, and convergence — Cauchy sequences, lim sup/inf
Sequences and series of functions — uniform convergence
Special functions: exponential, log, gamma, Fourier series
Multivariable calculus: inverse and implicit function theorems, differential forms
keyTakeaways: Every theorem in analysis has sharp hypotheses — weakening any one can break the result
Completeness of the reals is the foundation of calculus
Pointwise and uniform convergence are fundamentally different
Riemann integration has limitations that motivate Lebesgue theory
Stokes' theorem unifies multiple integral theorems in vector calculus
